title: Plain bagels
subtitle: Boiled then baked for a chewy crust and dense crumb
excerpt: Eight plain bagels made the New York way: shaped rings boiled briefly in malt-extract water before baking. The boiling step is what sets them apart from ordinary bread rolls, creating the shiny, snapping exterior and dense, chewy crumb.
sourceNotes: Ashkenazi Jewish baking tradition documented in pre-1928 American Jewish community cookbooks and settlement house cooking guides; the New York bagel bakery tradition is well-attested in early-twentieth-century municipal market records and community press. The boiling-then-baking method appears in American bakery manuals of the period, with roots in Ashkenazi bread-baking practice brought to the United States from Eastern Europe before the First World War.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze fully cooled. Slice before freezing and toast from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: After shaping, place rings on a floured tray, cover, and refrigerate overnight. Boil and bake straight from the fridge, adding 2 minutes to the bake time.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven. Conventional 230°C. Bake on a preheated tray or baking stone for the best bottom crust.

recipeTools.notes: A stand mixer makes the stiff dough easier to knead.

glossaryTerms.definition: Bagels are boiled for 30 to 60 seconds per side before baking. The hot water gelatinises the outer starch layer, setting a thin skin that bakes into the characteristic shiny, snapping crust. A longer boil gives a chewier, thicker crust; a shorter boil produces a thinner crust closer to a standard roll. Adding malt extract or bicarbonate of soda to the water deepens the colour and adds a slight malty flavour.

content.text: Bagels are shaped rings of low-hydration dough that go through two cooking stages:

that sets the crust, then a hot oven that colours and dries it. Eight bagels. The dough is stiffer than standard bread dough, which is what gives the dense, chewy crumb. Allow about 3 hours from start to finish.

Combine the flour, yeast, sugar, and salt in a large bowl. Add the water and mix until the dough comes together; it will be noticeably stiffer than a standard bread dough. Knead for 10 minutes by hand or 8 minutes with a dough hook, until smooth and firm. The dough should not be sticky. If it tears rather than stretches, it needs more kneading.
Cover the bowl and leave in a warm place for about 60 minutes, until the dough has roughly doubled. Line two baking trays with baking paper and dust with flour.

Divide the dough into 8 equal pieces, about 100 g each. Roll each piece into a rope about 25 cm long, then bring the ends together, overlapping by about 3 cm, and press firmly to seal. Place the rings on the lined trays. Cover loosely and leave for 20 minutes; they will puff slightly but should not double.

Heat the oven to 220°C fan (230°C conventional). Bring a large pan of water to a rolling boil and add the malt extract if using. Lower the bagels in 2 or 3 at a time and boil for 30 seconds per side. Lift out with a slotted spoon, drain briefly, and return to the lined trays. Bake for 20 to 25 minutes, until deep golden and firm. Cool on a wire rack for at least 15 minutes before cutting.
